contents: Anyone else feel a systemic fatigue (NOT tired from lack of sleep) when experiencing a flare up? An overall fatigue like being sick without any other symptoms besides IC related. All blood work normal.

to the IC Network. Yes --- unfortunately --- pain/discomfort can be more fatiguing than hard physical work. I do have other health issues, but I find that when I am in pain, my muscles seem to be tight and it's hard to relax. I see a pain management specialist and also did a sleep study. Getting my pain under control and sleeping with a BIPAP machine has been a real benefit for me. If you're experiencing frequent flares, I suggest you keep a detailed diary to help identify your triggers. Sending warm hugs, Donna

contents: I think many IC patients experience this -- we call it the "IC Belly." It's tough to get rid of unfortunately. I have not yet found an effective way to reduce it during flares, but I wish I could, because it's quite uncomfortable!!
signature: **** Jen *Diagnosed with severe IC in 2004 *Also diagnosed with PFD, fibromyalgia, chronic myofascial pain, IBS, migraines, allergies/asthma, dermatographism *Kept trying a million different treatments for all these things until I found what works, and I am doing okay these days with the help of a cocktail of medications and the InterStim, which was first placed in 2007. [I have had 2 revisions - one in 2010 when my battery died and had to be replaced, and one complete replacement (lead and generator) in 2012 after a fall on my stairs caused my lead to move.] *Current meds include Atarax (50mg at night), Lyrica (150mg twice a day), Xanax (0.5mg at night and as needed), Zanaflex (4mg at night), hydrocodone (10/325, every 6 hours as needed), Advair, Nasonex, Singulair (10mg at night), oral contraceptives, home instills containing Elmiron and Marcaine (as often as I need to do them). **I am not a medical authority nor do I offer definitive medical advice. I strongly encourage you to discuss your medical treatment with your personal medical care provider. Only they can, and should, give medical recommendations to you.

contents: Magnesium capsules have helped me & helps with the abdomin swelling i go every day now! I use (Country Life) Magnesium Caps 300mg each i take one a day! You will see results in a few days! Good Luck

The first thing I notice when a flare is starting is a puffy, swollen belly! I'm glad to find out that I'm not the only one that suffers from this. It stays swollen for the duration of the flare and them some. When I'm feeling normal, no flare, then my belly is flatter and the difference is amazing. Don't know what to do about it. I'll try cutting down on salt, but that's hard because sometimes the only way to make my food taste good is to salt it. I'm in a flare right now that just won't go away! I've been fighting this one for over a week with only minimal relief.Pain in the pelvic area, burning, swollen belly, feeling like I have to urinate all the time, it's just so depressing. Tried all my tricks to get rid of it and nothing seems to work this time. I finally used Benedryl today and got some relief for a few hours. Now it's back and I'm about ready to lose it! Barbie
I look like I'm 4 months pregnant all the time. Depressing on those summer days when you pull out your bathing suit. I've even had people ask my parents if I was "expecting". Oh great !
signature: Jen ...

contents: Did you try the 1teaspoon baking soda in a 8 oz glass of water? works in 5min for me Try it ! Check you diet.... Good Luck

Gently massaging/tickling with the tips of my fingers over my belly has a very relaxing effect, or having my fiance do it. My PT also use to take the palm of her hand and apply pressure to different areas of my belly a few seconds at a time. She said that as you apply pressure to a tense muscle, it will eventually give in and relax. My Pt was the first person to take a look at my belly. She said it was a big sign of IC and VV. I never have had a dr. look at my belly. Sharing-What does magnesium do for the belly?

Oh yeah, I get it too! In the beginning, right before and right at diagnosis, I was wondering why I was having to wear my PMS/period jeans all the time!!! Now I know why, and mine comes and goes with flares too, and shows up during PMS, as usual, ugh. Time to drag out the period pants again......LOL Tracey

contents: I am also soooo bloated There are certain foods/medicines that make it worse for me. Coffee and now I just found out antibiotics. I was on them for 7 days (had to do 10) and was ok, but on the 6th day I started to get sooo bloated, and by the evening I was bending over in so much pain. The next day I could barely urinate without screaming. So I am blaming the antibiotics. The nurse told me to discontinue it. I am better but still swollen.

I use (Country Life) Magnesium Caps 300mg each i take one a day! You will see results in a few days! Good Luck What kind of magnesium? I'm dying tonight. : (

Re: lower abdominal swelling The cause of the swelling is a bit of a mystery but most believe that it's caused by inflammation when the bladder gets irritated during the day... I used to get the belly all the time at first... but now I only get it if I eat a lot of white bread or products containing gluten. If I don't, I do so much better! Do you have our flare management guide? It has bladder wall rescue strategies that could also help! You can get the 40page flare guide by signing up for our free newsletter here! Interstitial Cystitis Weekly Newsletter From ICN https://www.ic-network.com/interstitial-cystitis-news/icn-e-newsletter/ ICN E-Newsletter Serves 30,000+ Patients Sign up today to receive the ICN's free E-Newsletter (Ezine), the oldest and most widely distributed electronic IC newsletter on the web today. Created in 1997, it is written for both IC patients and careproviders and offers breaking IC news stories, the latest IC research, consumer safety alerts, IC event calendar, Hugs & Welcome to the ICN! Jill PS. On magnesium... just know that your body can only tolerate a certain amount before it triggers loose bowel so if you are going to try it, start with a small amount first and work your way up. I don't really know of success using it though in my experience with patients.

contents: After sleeping for one hour, I woke up at 2 am feeling like my bladder is going to burst. I produced 40 ml of urine. The bladder sensation is still strong enough to keep me awake. Is there anything one can do (or take) in a situation like that to get some sleep? I took Amitriptyline at 1 am, and this makes not sleeping feel like torture... Thank you in advance.

I would reach out to your GP to see if anything else can help with the issue if you can. I know for me, heating pads help a ton for better sleep. I also cut way back on water at night, doing smaller sips. I still get up 1-2 times but it isn't as bad. I hope you find something that can help you. Hang in there!
One thing you could try is taking the amitriptyline earlier. What did you have for your evening meal? Any snacks or drinks after that? Any medications in the evening? Donna

contents: I took only Flomax/Tamsulosin and Amitriptyline. All I had eaten that day was broccoli. My last meal was over 4 hours before my flare began. One possible culprit is pushing the last few drops of urine during urination. Perhaps that is irritating something leading to a flare... "I know for me, heating pads help a ton for better sleep" Thank you for telling me about it. I will try to buy one tomorrow! I went to the ER. I wanted the doctor at the ER to expedite me visiting a urologist, seeing how I keep getting sleepless nights. The doctor surprised me by telling me that he believes that what I have is Prostatitis. He wants me to take a 6 week course of antibiotics. At first I was thrilled. Then I found out that all of the antibiotics used to treat this condition have tinnitus as a rare side effect. [Tinnitus makes me suicidal, so in my case risking getting tinnitus back is literally risking death.] To make matters worse, 90% of prostatitis patients don't have a bacterial infection that can be treated with antibiotics! Turns out that another name for chronic prostatitis is "chronic pelvic pain syndrom" and that both prostatitis and IC are under the umbrella of the Urinary Chronic Pelvic Pain Syndrome. So I am back to square one. Both IC and prostatitis are chronic conditions that the doctors can't easily explain and can't easily treat. It looks like the problem doesn't have to be with prostate for it to be called prostatitis (as 90% of chronic prostatitis patients don't have an infection in their prostate). Last edited by BillB; 01-28-2021, 06:27 PM.

It's not at all unusual for male IC patients to be first diagnosed as prostatitis. And yes, straining to get that last drop can be a problem. You could try sitting to urinate --- then just sit there and relax to see if that need to strain goes away. I have crossword books in my bathroom so I can do that --- and it helps. Donna

contents: "You could try sitting to urinate" Yes, it is certainly easier to urinate while sitting down. "see if that need to strain goes away." It is more of a habit. I will work on breaking the habit... Have an enjoyable weekend, Donna!

You could try breathing exercises to help you go rather than straining. Sometimes I have urinary retention and the breathing exercises can help. Sitting is always helpful for both genders, and as men get older they tend to need to do that anyway assuming they have prostrate surgery. Any form of Pelvic Floor Dysfunction from what I read is also helped by sitting.
Thank you Donna and ICGamer! In your opinion could something I eat cause a flare more than 12 hours later? Last edited by BillB; 01-30-2021, 02:01 PM.

I've had this condition for 2 or 3 years so take everything I say with a grain of salt. Sometimes it can be a pelvic floor issue *in my case both issues are happening simultaneously and i need to be more proactive*, and for me it seems like Flairs can last awhile, mine was on the 2nd Sunday this month. Through the elimination diet I have been able to figure out parts of the puzzle, but my symptoms didn't vanish yet *Sticking with the diet is the tricky part*. I'd say keep track of what you eat and drink, see if there is a trigger food at all. I hope you begin to feel a little better each day ! Last edited by ICGamer; 01-30-2021, 09:20 PM.

"elimination diet" Suppose one adds a food that triggers a flare to the diet. In your experience, how long does it take before one learns that it causes flares? Does one get a flare within several days of first trying the food, or does the flare come on within an hour or two?

My understanding is very limited arguably because I am trying to figure that aspect out myself. For me depending what I drink it can be within a couple hours. If you have something you think is causing the issue, take a break from it for about a week *just to rule it out that there isn't a build up so to speak*. I took Gatorade out about 3 days ago after figuring out it was probably the Red 40 and I have been feeling a bit better. I have heard everyone has a different IC experience though so take it with a grain of salt.
I eat identical food for two days, and one day/night is good and the following night is bad...

Sometimes other things can cause issues, I know in my case intimacy was a big issue and I also have PFD. I would talk to your urologist in greater detail about your symptoms. Perhaps make a symptom diary. There is a lot of overlap between some conditions like Pelvic Floor Dysfunction, so it is well worth talking about. Pelvic Floor Therapy can be very helpful but it all depends what is causing the issue.

Originally posted by BillB "elimination diet" Suppose one adds a food that triggers a flare to the diet. In your experience, how long does it take before one learns that it causes flares? Does one get a flare within several days of first trying the food, or does the flare come on within an hour or two? The time after an irritating food that the flare starts can vary. For me, if it's a liquid, it can hit as quickly as an hour --- and it can take a day or two. If I eat or drink something I shouldn't, I might be able to get away with a small amount, but if I add another possible irritant, even if it's a small amount, for me it can compound. I suggest you stick with the diet, as listed on this site, 100% for a while to see if that helps --- before you start trying other things. Donna

contents: "There's a link to diet information in my signature below. I suggest you use the information there to plan your diet." I have been doing that. Yesterday I at cucumbers, but still got a flare that prevented me from sleeping... Is it realistic to hope that my doctors will eventually agree to surgically remove my prostate and/or bladder? If so, does one need to suffer for more than a year before one is allowed to have that surgery?

I finally found something that might work: Pyridium. I was in a bad shape in the evening. It got down to where sleep was possible an hour or two after taking Pyridium. I believe a lower concentration of it is sold over-the-counter under the brand name AZO. Is there a downside to Pyridium that I am not aware of?

Yes, pyridium is available under the AZO name. I'm sure you already know it turns the urine a bright orange color --- and it stains anything that comes in contact with the urine. Vinegar will usually remove the stains from hard surfaces, but not from clothing. It sometimes stains contact lenses. I suggest you do a search for side effects, such as staining perspiration and other body fluids. It's usually prescribed on a short term basis to help with the pain of an infection. Since it helped you, it could help you to have instillations with lidocaine, marcaine (any of the "caines"). Some ICers do self instills regularly. Donna

contents: "Vinegar will usually remove the stains from hard surfaces", "staining perspiration" Thank you for letting me know! I will ask my doctor about marcaine instillations! Is it the case that you have never read about anyone suggesting that using pyridium or marcaine long-term might eventually damage one's bladder? Last edited by BillB; 02-02-2021, 06:24 PM.

I have never heard of it specifically damaged a bladder, but have heard about some who had the staining perspiration, tears, and even saliva -- I doubt you want to do that. I suggest you do a search for the side effects to get a total picture. Any one of the meds that end in caine could help -- if you have an instillation in your doctor's office and it offers temporary help, I suggest you ask about learning to do them yourself at home. Donna

contents: "I doubt you want to do that." I wouldn't mind any of that staining if I could be sure that can turn off that unpleasant sensation, especially when it interferes with my sleep. Last edited by ICNDonna; 02-04-2021, 09:42 AM.

hello, I am an Algerian women of 52 years. for now 4 months , i have terrible pain in the bladder. Some days are almost ok, but other are just horrible. I went to an urologist (actually many!) and they all wanted to do a cystoscopy. I was very afraid but finally agreed. so I had a cystoscopy. but the urologist dita simple one , without distension or biopsy, since he considered my bladder was showing no lesions and looked haelthy. he just filled it with more than 500ml of water and said he was happy about the capacity and quality of voiding. He concluded i had no IC. I dont know. When i read french lectures, they all say that you need hydrodistension with biopsy to rule out or confirm IC. is this still true? I am so afraid that i have to have a second cystoscopy with biopsy.

There are some medical professionals who still feel hydrodistention is the only way to diagnose interstitial cystitis, but many who are willing to make a diagnosis based on symptoms and ruling out other possible problems. There are some people with IC, including me, who experience relief for months after a hydrodistention. Even though this is a good option for me, I suggest trying other treatments before having it done. Normal bladder capacity, without anesthetic, is about 500 ml --- it can vary --- many IC patients have much lower, but that isn't always the case with IC. Some even have higher capacity. Have you tried doing an IC diet? Even though you don't have a diagnosis, I suggest you give it a try to see if it helps you feel better. I also suggest keeping a detailed diary for a few weeks to see if you can identify any things that cause you pain. And keep me posted about how you are doing. Donna

contents: yes i am trying it from the beginning but i keep having acid urine most of the time. can it be because a still drink milk and eat bread? or not enough vegetables? I surely don't want to do a hydrodistension. i just wanted to know if it was mandatory for the diagnosis, so as the biopsy. because my uro ruled IC out (I am convinced i have it though. my pain is clearly in my bladder) thanx for your kind response. have a nice day

I have to take antibiotics for a uti that’s resistant to the antibacs that don’t bother me. Does anyone know of prelief or anything helps? I’m getting some decent burning so I just have an ice pack and tried taking an antihistamine. Thank you!!

In a similar position - I have come down with serious Strep Throat infection and I will need to take Amoxycillin for 10 days 500mg / 2x a day - obviously terrified bc antibiotics have always triggered my IC, which is currently in remission. Not sure what to do but I know my life is about to go back to being hellish again if I take this stuff, but if I don't the strep could move into my heart... looking for suggestions

The best suggestion I can think of is to discuss your concerns with your doctor --- it may be that antibiotics are the best choice, but you need to weigh the potential harm against the possible good before you make your decision. Back in the days before there was such a thing as antibiotics some people did survive some serious infections, but far more recover since antibiotics were developed. I do suggest making sure cultures are done to be sure it's the correct medication for the specific "bug" involved. Sending healing wishes, Donna

contents: Re: Sharing What do you do to help prevent a flare? Stay on the medication that supresses my periods and avoid getting too rough during intimacy. That's all I need to avoid most flare-ups these days. And --- if you have a flare, what's your most successful treatment? Marinol, which is THC in a pill. (It's expensive, so if you can legally get weed where you live, I recommend using the plant to make edibles instead.) Honorable mentions, in order of effectiveness:Heat from a heating pad or hot water (in the shower or bath) Kratom (legal in some places, but not others—look up your local laws) Rest Massage from a hand-held massager Sitting on the toilet for a few hours playing a handheld video game How can you stay on your diet when eating with others in their homes? ... in a restaurant? ... when traveling? The IC diet doesn't work for me, so I'm not the right person to answer these questions, but I hope others will share. Last edited by green_the_fish; 08-25-2018, 04:10 AM. Reason: Mods, is discussing kratom OK here? Please advise
signature: Chronic conditions: IC, bipolar disorder, Lown-Ganong-Levine syndrome, Raynaud's disease, bile reflux, scoliosis Current IC treatments: menstrual suppression Daily treatments for other conditions: Neurontin, Zyprexa, Cymbalta, Modafinil As-needed treatments for other conditions: Klonopin, Ambien

contents: Good morning all, I have urodynamics testing coming up on Thursday sometime. My IC has been not in remission per se, but I've haven't felt any symptoms here recently. My only BIG concern is with this test I do have to be catheterized. I know it's only going to be for a short period of time, but knowing me, my body HATES catheters. Thankfully, my urologist when he say the look of terror on my face, he then told me YES, they will use a pediatric catheter for me so as to avoid triggering an IC flare. IC symptoms aren't really why this test is being done. I was just recently diagnosed with spina bifida occulta(which usually is diagnosed in people much younger). And the problem with that is, the nerves are able to fire, but the "signal" is not getting through from my bladder to my brain if that makes sense. Anybody have any suggestions with coping with discomfort? I can't have narcotics prior to so not quite sure what I'm going to do. Thanks.
signature: Diagnosed IC/PBS 4/20/2016 by cysto with hydrodistention(2/20/2016) PFD diagnosed 6/2016/ruling out either Crohn's disease or Ulcerative colitir Update: Have Chiari 1 malformation, hydrocephalus-currently recovering from VP shunt placement on 1/3/2018. Also diagnosed with spina bifida occulta(causes urinary retention-currently working on a solution-accidentally found on 9/13/2017) Possibility of either occult tethered cord or tethered cord. Current meds: Elmiron 400 mg(2 pills in AM, 2 at bedtime) Benadryl 50 mg as needed at bedtime(wasn't prescribed for IC originally but does a good job of keeping it in check). Zonegran 200 mg once daily at bedtime(for migraine) Neurontin 300 mg once daily at bedtime(dosage changed to 900 mg/divided 3 times daily. Elavil 25 mg p.o once daily at bedtime. Zoloft 50 mg p.o once daily at bedtime Norco 5/325 mg p.o 4 times daily as needed for pain after neurosurgery Zanaflex 2-4 mg p.o every 6 hours p.r.n muscle spasms(works meh)

contents: Re: A little bit anxious about something today I would definitely get something you can take right before to calm you down. Even 1 mg of Lorazipam would do it. In small doses Lorazipam stops muscle spasms. I take it for Urethritis .5mg before bed. It helps with urethral spasms which you can get by using a catheter. I know they use a numbing gel on the area. There really shouldn't be actual pain. Maybe an antispasmodic for a few days after the test. I take generic Ditropan. Good luck with your test. It's never as bad as you imagine. I can imagine some horrible things.

Re: A little bit anxious about something today Thanks for calming me down Cindi. Much appreciated this morning. I do actually have access to Zanaflex which is prescribed for my pelvic floor spasms. Unfortunately, I can't take Ditropan because my urologist doesn't want me to take anything that might further slow down the "works" in his words. And yes, I can also imagine some horrible things as well.

contents: Re: A little bit anxious about something today If you don't get urethral spasms you shouldn't have any problems. Some extra Zanaflex for a day or two after might be helpful. I'm always terrified about any procedures so I always get something to calm me down. If you're pretty brave you won't need anything. It sounds like you have good communication with your doctor. You could always give him a call to voice your concerns.

Re: A little bit anxious about something today The best suggestion I can think of is to ask your doctor about taking something immediately after the test --- they might even be able to give you a shot if you're painful. And try to relax when they insert the catheter. When I'm having anything from a catheter to a blood draw, I concentrate on deep, slow breathing and consciously relaxing my muscles. Please be sure to let us know how it goes for you. Warm hugs, Donna

contents: Re: A little bit anxious about something today I've had urodynamics and compared to other tests it's very doable. Basically, they'll just slowly fill your bladder and ask you questions about what you feel.... like "when do you first feel an urge to urinate" etc. etc. I would look your doctor in the eye and ask for a safe word... and if it gets super uncomfortable, ask him to stop immediately. And, yes, having some medication for pain afterwards would be reasonable though it shouldn't last long at all. Really, it's pretty quick. Jill

contents: Although I am not officially diagnosed with IC due to poor quality urologists, I have been dealing with IC symptoms for the last 8 months and believe I have it. I have been taking oxybutynin since May which really helped me feel normal again. My main symptoms were urgency, frequency, and pressure and they became almost non existent except for when I had sex (I would feel pressure to pee during penetration but not at any other time). I had a small flare that lasted about 2 days in September when I had an alcoholic cocktail on my fiance's birthday. That was my first real flare after I started taking oxybutynin and my first time drinking alcohol since my symptoms started. Two days ago (October 3rd), I went swimming at the local rec centre. I hadn't gone in a pool since last December (before I started having symptoms) and no more than 10 minutes after being in the water, I started to have the same pressure and urgency as I did before I started taking oxybutynin. Its been two days now and I now have all 3 of the main symptoms I had before as well as the sensation of swelling in my urethra and some itching. Sometimes surges of pain too. I've been using AZO these last few days and continuing the oxybutynin but it isn't helping much. I've also read on what to do when having a flare but haven't found much that is specific to my situation. Diet does not affect me, other than alcohol, and I do not typically have burning. Basically, my ulimtate questions are: What should I do to help lessen the length of this flare? How long do flares induced by chlorine typically last? What can I do to help subside the symptoms while I go through the flare? Help and suggestions are greatly appreciated..thank you in advance!

Re: Tips for chlorine induced flare? The things I think of are drinking enough to help clear the chlorine out of your urethra. A sitz bath with Epsom salts can be soothing. Adding a little baking soda to a glass of water might help. I think time is probably the best thing in your favor. If you swim again, you could try liberally applying Vaseline to your urethra. And absolutely don't sit around in a wet swim suit --- best to shower immediately after swimming, and put on dry clothes. I hope this flare goes away quickly. Donna

contents: Hi Do you take prescription meds everyday to prevent your pain. I have prescription Flavoxate for bladder pain. Very Confused!

Re: meds on a daily basis First of all, welcome to the IC Network! Yes, I do. I take Elavil daily for nerve pain associated with IC, Myrbetriq for spasms which also cause pain, Uribel for mild flares, and Percocet when it gets really bad. What is your confusion? I'd love to help if I can.
signature: Diagnosed with severe IC and Endometriosis in 2016 Medications: Elmiron, Elavil, Myrbetriq, Uribel, Valium, Baclofen, Percocet Surgeries: Laparoscopy, Hydrodistention (2) Procedures: Regular bladder instillations Therapies: Pelvic Floor Physical Therapy 2x/week, homeopathic home-care, yoga and massage Helpful Links: Patient Help: http://www.ic-network.com/patientlinks.html IC Diet: http://www.ic-network.com/patient-re...-introduction/

contents: Which product is better to try for IC pain? Neuroquell or the Prosirona? Thanks.

Re: prosirona versus neuroquell I had to google both and read about them. My honest opinion is that I would not try either one in my case. If you have mild IC-maybe. I have tried Voltaran gel, which is a prescription strength antiinflammatory that is applied topically, it does not penetrate deep enough to get to the bladder. I use it for muscle pain in my shoulders and my back, and it helps those areas slightly. I know some people get relief from herbal products, but they make my pain worse. Except Desert Harvest aloe, that is the only product I have tried that did not make my bladder pain worse. Of course, it didnt help- but I dont get sick as often so I am still taking it. If these products offer a money back guarantee that can be trusted, then try it and let us know if it helps.
signature: I have basically done every treatment for IC except cyclosporine and botox. Everything has failed. I live on pain meds all day. My diet is so strict that I now have multiple vitamin deficiencies. I have also tried numerous alternative therapies. I would do a cystectomy, but doctors seem to think living in pain is better. Currently taking Synthroid, Elmiron, Percocet, Toradol, Desert Harvest aloe. Interstim done 4 times, what a waste. Also newly diagnosed with Hunners Ulcers on cysto. Now disabled due to IC. A body of life, a bladder of hell.

contents: Re: prosirona versus neuroquell I have never taken either. If you're not following an IC diet, I suggest that as the first step. And there's a link to AUA Guidelines in my signature below that I suggest you read. Donna

contents: Re: prosirona versus neuroquell I have to give a recommendation for DH Aloe too. Already on hydroxyzine,I started the DH Aloe 2 weeks ago. I only tried it because I was hoping to lower my hydroxyzine dose (it makes me sleepy). I am now sorry that I never tried it sooner. On a scale of 1-10,10 being most pain/twitchy bladder irritability I am a 2 on hydroxyzine. After adding DH Aloe,I am about a zero almost all the time. I tried a cinnamon roll at the fair(I can get by with some cinnamon) and I did have bladder burning, but only about 50% of the usual flare. I am very pleased.And I should add that I am not a big believer in natural supplements.

Re: prosirona versus neuroquell I have tried all treatments - amitriptyline, hydroxyzine, elmiron, cystoprotek, cystorenew, desert harvest aloe, various different bladder instillations, interstim by Dr. Peters and Botox injections by Dr. Peters. Absolutely nothing helped and Dr. Peters has nothing left to offer me but cyclosporine and I am scared to death to try that. I have also done pelvic floor PT by three different PTs. No relief from anything. I am now trying a good brand CBD oil by my naturopath but does not seem to be helping. So frustrated. I need to find something to help. I have had IC for 28 years and it is progressing.

Re: prosirona versus neuroquell I have failed everything, too except cyclosporine, which I am afraid to try. Just started on pain meds which help some. I am thinking of trying acupuncture, but I am not getting my hopes up. My doc says I am the only patient with IC he has had that did not respond to some sort of treatment.

Re: prosirona versus neuroquell I have found acupuncture somewhat helpful-and my accupuncturist will treat me for stress at the same time,which is helpful (if I recall,the spots are ear & top of the head for stress).You have been through the wringer Holly-hoping that something will bring you relief.hugs,shorty

I'm curious, during a flare, whether it is better to hold it in pain/discomfort until the bladder is closer to being full or to pee every 30min to an hour when my bladder wants to do that? Also drink a lot andnpee a lot or drink less and pee less? I know everyone is different but I'm curious about the strategies folks use.
Re: To pee or not to pee! One thing I have found is that if I don't drink enough, my urine becomes concentrated and more irritating. Donna

signature: UTI 3/2015 IC ,PFD prescribed meds: Endep 60mg, Elmiron 400mg, BC. Enablex 7.5mg. Supplements: Chitomur peptide, colostrum, Echinacea, liver detox swisse, calcium citrate, glucosamine, Psyllium Husk. Daily diet: Breakkie- oats with chia seeds and no fat milk. contents: Re: To pee or not to pee! I ussally drink lots anyways, I oee frequently but I do better when my urine is dilute. For a flare I drink more, I hope that it helps flush out what set me off providing it was food.
signature: My are with you all. May you all find a way to peace and joy in your lives.

contents: Re: To pee or not to pee! I'm drinking and peeing a lot. I do notice that if I take benedryl at night and sleep well without waking up to go my day will be significantly better. Idk if this is because I manage to get a full bladder or the antihistamine effects or both.

Re: To pee or not to pee! Originally posted by cazattack I'm drinking and peeing a lot. I do notice that if I take benedryl at night and sleep well without waking up to go my day will be significantly better. Idk if this is because I manage to get a full bladder or the antihistamine effects or both. I suspect it's both --- getting adequate sleep can be important. Donna

contents: Hi, I'm coming down off a week long flare and wanted to share a tip. When I notice a flare not getting better on its own in a couple days, I switch from my normal filtered tap water to bottles of Evian. It has proved to be very helpful for me and I know others drink it as well. I'd recommend adding it to your flare toolbox.
signature: Current Meds: Nortriptyline (35 mg), Hydroxyzine (50 mg), Cephalexin (250 mg before sexual activity) As Needed: Prelief, AZO, Evian Water, Aleve

contents: Hello all. I had cysto/hydro done yesterday needless to say I was in severe pain afterwards. I haven't had this done in 14years. My uro wanted to see if there was any change to the bladder. There was no change I still had the hunners ulcers and she said they bled horribly while she was looking at the bladder and she had to stop. Up until yesterday I had my IC at bay. I take 50mg of ultam (a pain pill that was described to me as a really high Tylenol, like 800mg) and I also take Neurontin. She wants me to try Elmiron again after several years of not taking it I got so sick in the stomach I had to quit it after 2mos. The problem is they consider the Ultram an opiate and some docs do not want to give it anymore, so I have to go to pain management. I am horribly upset. I have a chronic condition with this IC like we all do... now what was working for me my uro wants to change. Has anybody else had their pain meds taken away? and how do I handle this?

Re: Flare from hydro yesterday! Im so sorry that the hydro caused a flare Ive not heard that Ultrams are opiates. I dont think they need to bother people like those of us with unrelenting pain as far as our pain meds go. Youre certainly going to need the pain meds while youre in severe pain. Has your uro suggested Elmiron instills? Ive heard that works well for some if they cant tolerate oral Elmiron. Sending you healing thoughts dear <
signature: Diagnosed 1997 Elmiron 2X daily, Hydroxizine 2X Daily, MSM&Glucosamine 1500 mg daily, Ditropan IC diet Bladder retraining when flaring

contents: Re: Flare from hydro yesterday! No she has not suggested Elmiron instills maybe I can ask her about them? Thanks so much for replying.

Re: Flare from hydro yesterday! It doesn't sound at all like she is trying to take away your pain medications by suggesting a pain management specialist. Doctors have to follow some pretty strict rules in the management of pain --- many specialties don't include being able to manage long term pain by prescribing pain medications. A doctor can lose his/her license by ordering too many narcotic medications. I see a pain management specialist and he gave me back my life. Many, if not most, doctors' privileges do include the management of temporary pain, such as post operative, a broken bone, etc., and your doctor should have ordered something for you following your hydrodistention. My pain is from thoracic nerve damage created during surgery to remove cancer tissue from my spine, but pain is pain and keeping my IC pain pretty well controlled is an excellent side effect from back pain treatment. It's not unusual to have pain for a few days after a hydro. I hope after you have time to heal you will have some relief. Sending warm well wishes, Donna

contents: Re: Flare from hydro yesterday! Hydros are about the only thing that will take me out of a flare.... Mine just keep getting worse and worse till I have a hydro done. I am in pain for a while after hydro because of the stretching of the bladder. My Dr gives me ultram( tramadol) after my hydros. Percocet makes me constipated and screws with my IBS. Try to stay in a good place mentally and I hope you feel relief soon

Hey friends, I've been doing pretty well with managing my IC on-and-off for more than a year now,and I've got a really lovely understanding partner whom I've been with for about a year and a half (woah!). I am having a really, really hard time getting my libido back up, though; it's never recovered from before I had IC. I used to enjoy sex and, while I didn't have a tonne of it, I liked it and sought it out from partners happily. Now it feels like a tonne of work to even work up the idea of wanting it, even with a partner that I love and trust and am attracted to. I think it's because I'm always afraid it will hurt (and it often does a little bit) and that totally turns me off. So I'm thinking of trying to get a book to help me out with this; something to do with IC and dealing with painful sex. What books do y'all recommend? I am thinking of "When Sex Hurts" by Dr. Goldstein, or "Silent Suffering", but I'm open to suggestions! What has worked for you guys?
Have any of you tired Oil of Oregano, which is antibacterial, antiseptic and anti fungal for IC and if so have any of you had any sucess with it ?

Re: Oil of oregano I use it for my irritable bowel and find it great for that. Not sure if it helps my bladder but does not hurt it so worth a try for sure. The one i have is enteric coated with peppermint oil and caraway seed. But i have tried oregano oil on its own also and it didn't hurt my bladder at all so definitely worth a try. Hope this helps a bit

contents: I have mentioned that I get intermittent groin pain to my urologist in the past & he wasn't too impressed by it. He thinks maybe it is muscular from my PFD. I am waiting till Aug. for an appt. with a new physical therapist, but I'm wondering if anyone else has experienced this type of discomfort & what you do for it. I have been doing so well on my amitriptyline & this pain just started up this week(haven't had it for months). It's not there in the morning & starts up around now, the late afternoon until bedtime. I don't remember if anyone has had this. Thanks.
I get it I also get groin pain. Mostly I have to breathe through it. But ICE is my friend most days. So I ice the heck out of it.
Thanks icinak!
i get it too, everything from below my belly button all the way to my whole vaginal/pelvic area and around to my low back. my whole "area" throbs and aches too.
signature: my name is Katie, 22 yr old mother & nursing student. I want to be a writer and midwife. I have severe IC w/ hunner ulcers and urethritis with lesions ENDO, chronic pelvic pain, PFD adhesions, scoliosis, arthritis,migraines,asthma chronic pain. Mother to a beautiful little girl. She has my heart and she's everything to me. Thinking positive and consciously untensing tense muscles helps a ton!!!! physical therapy also helps a lot.

contents: I get this sort of pain too, at least what I would call groin pain. I will be walking along just fine and then suddenly I get a terrible shooting pain from the top of my leg into my pelvis. My PT worked with me on some exercises to help this, mine is from PFD. I have had this for years, when it happens I can't do anything but limp along. My stretches have helped some, but I still get the pain Sandra

contents: Hi KA For six yrs now since the onset of my IC symptoms I have experienced bladder and pelvic pain, but it is just within the past couple months that I have experienced groin pain...the superficial muscle pain that I have been complaining of recently in my possibly "fibro???" thread....you have responded in that thread (thank you) so I am sure you remember what I am talking about. As that all over body front and back superficial muscle pain begins to gradually subside from the really intense first day pain of these superficial muscle pain flares I've been having, the pain seems to settle in on the superficial part of my groin muscles very heavily, like a weight, with intense pain w/ the lightest touch, and that particular pain in the groin area stays around the longest and is the LAST pain to go away when the body "flare" has finished on day 5. Just sharing my experience with the 5 and counting pain episodes such as this that I have experienced so far two weeks apart, 4-5 days in length on the dot each time over the past couple of months.... I have PFD, VV, Vulvodynia, IC....but I just cannot find any connection between the body pain and groin pain I have been having as related to any of my dxd conditions. The only thing I can think of for me with the groin pain is that my pelvic and bladder areas are already compromised, and possibly that is why the pain sets in there hard from the begininng and is the last thing to go away. Other than that I am clueless. This is probably not of much help??!! If nothing else I can commiserate on having groin pain!!! Well, the groin pain I feel is not intermittent except that it appears for just the 4-5 days of these pain episodes I've been having, and during those episodes it is constant and the same, not shooting or here and there. Keep me posted on how things go for you. I hope you find answers and relief soon!

I get it occasionally especially the day after I have had intercourse. I suppose it could be PFD. Since developing IC I ahve had all sorts of weird pains down there. I guess it's just part of IC and getting older even though I am only 30. I wish I could figure it all out. Lately every day is new adventure for me in the pain department.
signature: Rebekah - Diagnosed with IC and Hunner's Ulcers in March 2009 Two Hunner's Ulcers were removed by laser surgery surgery Jan 15 , 2010 Proud Mother of Jeremy Jr. Born on Wed May 4, 2011 "For every door that is closed a new one is opened" The Lord's Prayer Our Father, who art in heaven, Hallowed be thy Name. Thy kingdom come. Thy will be done, On earth as it is in heaven. Give us this day our daily bread. And forgive us our trespasses, As we forgive those who trespass against us. And lead us not into temptation, But deliver us from evil. [For thine is the kingdom, and the power, and the glory, for ever and ever.] Amen.

contents: Thanks girls! It helps to know that others get this type of pain too. Bri, This groin pain I am having is atypical of my fibro pain. My uro said to tell my rheumatologist, which I did. She prescribed flexeril, which I am trying for the first time tonight. It's always something, isn't it?

Flexeril 10 mg is what I have been given so far for my pain, Karen Anne. I really can't say if it has helped in any way except that it makes me sleep but I still wake up in pain in the morning. I can't take it during the daytime b/c of that sleepy side effect, so don't know if it would help with my pain during daytime. This last episode I did not have any Flexeril left to take at night, and my pain experience was exactly the same as when I did have it to take at night. So for me I am guessing it does not work for whatever is going on w/ my body!!! But I do know many others take it with much success for PFD and other various muscular pain issues, so I hope it helps you, too. Keep us posted on how it goes, ok?
I also get the pain! I've found that ice alternated with heat helps me.
groin pain I have diagnosed IC for many years and never had groin pain, now it is really bad and only on the left side. Thanks to all that posted, it is helpful to know it is probably IC related.

Re: Does anyone get groin pain? I know it has been years since your last post. I also have IC, and experience groin pain on my left side as well. I was just "googling" about groin pain when I saw your post. It is so uncomfortable and can't stand it! I recently started taking Elmiron. I know it takes a while for it to work. I have had a few headaches and stomach issues, but I'm not sure if it's from the Elmiron. Not sure if you'll see this, but if you do wondering if you're still experiencing the groin pain?

Hello fellow ICers I wanted to share this information with you because in the last few weeks I have had some very nasty flares and I have pinpointed it to the facial cleanser I have been using. I have sensitive skin and I have used the same cleanser for the last 15 years but sadly for me they changed the formula and I no longer find it suitable, so that caused me to go shopping for a new cleanser. Ouch Ouch Ouch!! Is all I can say. I used a sensitive brand and the pain was nearly unbearable. My pain went off the scale. I stopped the cleanser the pain went I started the cleanser again the pain returned. I think it is really important to share this info because some of you could be flaring because of cosmetics etc. I know how frustrating it is to be in a flare when you're doing all the right things. But it could be a case of what you are putting on your skin. (Same goes for medically prescribed creams). I had been using a vitamin cream on my face 12 on this ago and it was dong the same thing. So it's worth thinking about what creams, lotions, cleansers etc you are using. With so many anti ageing serums, creams etc we could be loading our systems with IC irritating ingredients ie vitamins, acids ie glycolic, aha's, bha's etc . Consider your cosmetics girls. Hope this post helps someone out there. Hugs Stacey.

Re: Cosmetics, Creams and Facial Cleanser cause me to flare Stacey, WOW! you are exactly right. i am allergic to Propylene Glycol and Polyethelene Glycol, both chemicals in many facial products, lotion, soaps, etc. i have found they are absorbed and make my bladder burn. Have you pinpointed the chemical in your cleanser that was bothering you so you can avoid it? Propylene/Polyethelene Glycol is also in some foods and many medications. It was in a blood pressure medicine I was taking and it really helped me to get off it. We have to be detectives to fire out what causes our pain. Jo Ann

Re: Cosmetics, Creams and Facial Cleanser cause me to flare Hi Jo Ann, No I have not pin pointed the exact ingredient yet but I will try to do that next. Burning is a good description ,the flare was so much more severe, than say if I ate something I shouldn't have. I have also found medications oral and creams etc cause pain. It is not really something that people talk about is it? Sleuths we must be. Stacey. I will never use a cosmetic again without checking first for these ingredients. Thank you so much for your help and info. Exactly what the forum was created for to help each other with information and support. Big big hugs to you. Stacey. Re: Cosmetics, Creams and Facial Cleanser cause me to flare Stacey, these chemicals are in so many things. I went through my house with a magnifier and looked at the ingredients on everything I used and consumed. I had to throw out many, many things. Check all your prescription medicenes too. Also many fast food places (like McDonalds) put Propylene Glycol in their foods. I never eat those places anymore. To me their food is like poison. Keep spreading the word. I think many people have trouble with all these chemicals and just don't know it . Jo Ann

Not sure where to post this but why does taking antibiotics help with my ic? My doc doesn't know why. All I know is when I'm on it I feel better, my lower back doesn't hurt anymore, the jabbing pains in my sides stop and I pee a bigger amount. 1 week after going off of it ...boom! It all starts in again.
Re: Anyone know why it helps? it seems to be because antibiotics act as potent anti-inflammatories.
signature: When there is research, there are answers. When there is persistence, there are solutions. Don't stop looking for answers; don't give up.

contents: Re: Anyone know why it helps? It is probably because IC is in all likelihood is caused by some sub-clinical infection that is not able to be detected easily. I know some people claim that antibiotics have anti-inflammatories in them, but this is NOT true about all antibiotics. I also get a lot of relief when I am on antibiotics. In fact I was on them low dose for like over 4 years and it healed my bladder( this was physically proven through cystoscopy). I had suffered for like a year and a half in extreme constant pain in my bladder. I had a regular cysto done and that is when I was diagnosed. The doctor said my bladder was severely inflamed and vascular. Well I had started doing bladder instillations and that helped a little but then I started getting all these UTI's from doing the instillations. I got a real nasty UTI that would not go away. I ended up on antibiotics for like 6 to 8 weeks just to kill it. After being on the antibiotics for like 3 weeks my bladder felt better than it ever did since getting IC. I had a scheduled apt with a urologist to check things out and he said my bladder looked very good, no inflammation really visibly. He even agreed that the antibiotics did help me. He was not sure how, but the proof was in the pudding. Unfortunately I could not stay on them and sure enough very soon after going off them my bladder is back in hell again. I even called the manufacturer of the antibiotic I was taking at the time ( Keflex) just to see what anti-inflammatory they had in there because I was also reading stuff about antibiotics having anti-inflammatory properties. Well when I finally got through to the person I asked them and they told me there were No anti-inflammatory properties in the antibiotic at all. I was told that a lot of people mistakenly think there is because they feel better when they take them. She said that what is happening actually is that you take the antibiotic and it starts to kill off the bacteria and the inflammation goes down. This is why people feel better on them. I also will say that I have taken anti-inflammatories for my IC and they did not help no where near like the antibiotics do! That alone tells me it is the antibiotic itself that is helping the bladder, probably by killing stuff off. Originally posted by waterflow Not sure where to post this but why does taking antibiotics help with my ic? My doc doesn't know why. All I know is when I'm on it I feel better, my lower back doesn't hurt anymore, the jabbing pains in my sides stop and I pee a bigger amount. 1 week after going off of it ...boom! It all starts in again.

Re: Anyone know why it helps? jen74- If you were on antibiotics for four years and did well wouldn't that long of a time have killed off the bacteria for good? Do you think it may be an unusual strain that hasn't been identified and came back when you went off the long term antibiotics?

Re: Anyone know why it helps? http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2801304/ an article on the anti-inflammatory affects of anti-biotics. but this is not to say in some instances there might be a sub-clinical infection. since it is thus far impossible to prove or disprove the theory of a subclinical infection except through the proof of people on long term anti-biotics feeling better. it is possible that in some instances it is the case. it is also possible the anti-inflammatory properties gave the time and space for the bladder to heal. maybe they have anti-histamine affects in some people. i have never heard this, but everyone can get some weird side effect from a medicine and maybe that is one that some people get (and it's good)? totally talking through my nose. like i said i just thought about this! Last edited by makamala; 02-02-2015, 04:29 AM.
signature: When there is research, there are answers. When there is persistence, there are solutions. Don't stop looking for answers; don't give up.

contents: Re: Anyone know why it helps? Earthlady- yeah, it is very strange. My Primary care doctor kind of thinks that there are bacteria in the bladder wall. When we take antibiotics it is able to kill some of the bacteria, but not all. It kind of keeps them at bay. I also think there is definitely an immune issue going on as well. I know many people who have Chronic Lyme disease also get positive results from being on antibiotics long term, but when they go off of them, their pain eventually returns. I really don't have the answer to why the antibiotics do work for quite a bit of people who have IC. I wish I did. Also I remember one doctor telling me a long time ago that once a bacteria finds it way into the bladder, it is like there is a path going there from then on. The way he explained it was that once bacteria find the bladder, it is like they will continue to go there. That is why lots of women when they get their first UTI, 9 times out of 10, they will get another one at some point. There just has to be something to it. Another thing that I found was interesting was I was on the Keflex for all that time and I did ok with it. However, out of nowhere it stopped working. I mean it just would not work anymore. My doctor at the time switched me to augmentin and that worked as well. Unfortunately I could not stay on that one due to having side effects. This is why I kind of debunk the whole antibiotics having anti-inflammatory properties. If that were the case, then the Keflex would not stop working right? I really hope one day the researchers can really look into the whole pathogen/bacteria connection to IC. I think they need to open up that file again and look a bit closer. I think IC is very complex though. I mean it may be more than just the bladder that is involved. Makamala- There probably are some antibiotics that do have anti-inflammatory properties. I just know when I discovered I was getting better on the Keflex I called the makers to find out what it was that was in it that was making me feel better. I also thought maybe there was some sort of anti-inflammatory in it, but I was told no. It is very baffling though. I know quite a few women who have IC that all admit they felt way better when on antibiotics. There has to be something more to it. Originally posted by earthlady jen74- If you were on antibiotics for four years and did well wouldn't that long of a time have killed off the bacteria for good? Do you think it may be an unusual strain that hasn't been identified and came back when you went off the long term antibiotics?

Re: Anyone know why it helps? Unfortunately, antibiotics not only kill off the "bad" bacteria, they can also kill of the normal, healthy bacteria, especially if used full time, long term. The only time I will take them is if there's a diagnosed infection working. That is my opinion only --- and I know there are some people out there who feel they are helping --- what we have to do is to look at all the options and make our decisions based on whether the potential for good, weighed against the potential for flares. Sending encouraging hugs, Donna

contents: Re: Anyone know why it helps? jen74, it is all a great mystery. i hope you find a way back to feeling good again. you are in my thoughts! there was a study with keflex but i can only read an abstract. from the abstract all i can gauge is it showed keflex did not have anti-inflammatory properties at the concentration they used. the abstract is not helpful at all for me. i wish we could read the whole article. http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/3755739 i hope you find something that has less side effects and you get rid of your pain! since nothing seems to be working. have you considered manzanita? it is in the same family as uva ursi. it has 16 anti-biotic compounds vs uva-ursi's 2. when nothing else works you have nothing to lose. it is hard to find. but you never know!
signature: When there is research, there are answers. When there is persistence, there are solutions. Don't stop looking for answers; don't give up.

contents: Re: Anyone know why it helps? Makamala- Thanks for the support! I have tried Uvaursi in the past but not Manzanita. Would this be a herb that I would find at like an herb store? I am interested in trying it. I also right now have an actual UTI that was diagnosed( ecoli). I tend to get them and would like to try something to prevent them also. Having an ecoli UTI on top of an already inflamed bladder really sucks big time. Have you even taken the manzanita? Originally posted by makamala jen74, it is all a great mystery. i hope you find a way back to feeling good again. you are in my thoughts! there was a study with keflex but i can only read an abstract. from the abstract all i can gauge is it showed keflex did not have anti-inflammatory properties at the concentration they used. the abstract is not helpful at all for me. i wish we could read the whole article. http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/3755739 i hope you find something that has less side effects and you get rid of your pain! since nothing seems to be working. have you considered manzanita? it is in the same family as uva ursi. it has 16 anti-biotic compounds vs uva-ursi's 2. when nothing else works you have nothing to lose. it is hard to find. but you never know!

Re: Anyone know why it helps? i have not taken manzanita. i have taken uva ursi. uva ursi did help me. it will be hard to find manzanita. i have no idea why it's so hard to find! you should definitely find an herbalist who will work with you and they should know how to get it. you might be able to find tinctures in glycerine but in my experience with herbals using the whole leaf has been better. amazingly amazon doesn't have it and neither does mountain rose. i don't get it. it is not a poison. lol. maybe call mountain rose and ask them if they have any idea. also maybe you can grow your own and ask an herbalist to explain to you how to use the plant. good luck!

contents: So I really thought I was in a permanent remission, with no significant flares in nearly five years. I was sure that menopause had cured me! But for some reason I am starting to flare again. Getting up at night a few times, pain, burning, etc. Nothing like what I had in the past, but I'm really nervous. I also have rheumatoid arthritis and was actually in a good enough place with my IC that I was able to take naprosyn for it on a regular basis (I tried that once before when my IC was still active and my RA was first diagnosed and learned that wasn't a good idea for me!) But then I hurt my knee pretty badly last summer, and started taking more naprosyn, so I'm wondering if that may have been the culprit in this recurrence. I've cut that out, but it's been hard as I miss it for the RA. Anyway.... sorry for rambling. My real question is what I should try over the counter for some help with the pain and burning. Has anything changed in the last few years? Before my doctor prescribed Pyridium on an ongoing basis I used AZO and Uristat (they quit helping after a while) and Cystex (never worked really well for me). Is there anything new out there? I would love to hear about it. In the meantime I'm relying on my bed buddy, baking soda and trying to keep my diet really clean. Thanks so much for any advice!

Re: Ugh, flaring after years in remission - any new OTC helps out there? I know there is a medication called Uribel that can help. It turns your pee blue. It has like 4 different things in it, one is an antispasmodic, it has an anti-inflammatory, and an antiseptic. It would worth a try to see if it will help. You need a prescription for it though.

Re: Ugh, flaring after years in remission - any new OTC helps out there? So sorry to hear you're flaring after all that time. That is so awesome that you were in remission for so long, though. If you got there once, you can do it again! I use uribel and klonopin when I'm flaring. Amitriptyline also helps me. I don't have burning, but I do have pressure and frequency. Is Naprosyn the same as Naproxen (Aleve)? If so, I have found that I can take that a few times during my period and it doesn't affect my bladder. If I took it on an ongoing basis, it might, though.
signature: Jane Diagnosed 1/7/14 via cysto/hydro Current Treatment: 10 mg amitriptyline, DH Aloe Vera Used During Flares: Uribel, extra DH Aloe Vera, Klonopin and Tramadol (as needed) Practicing bladder training and stress control (hot baths, pelvic stretching) Recently tried PT, do not appear to have PFD Things that have not helped in the past: Oxybutynin, Vesicare, Pyridium, Myrbetriq, Vagifem Also have migraines and IBS

contents: Re: Ugh, flaring after years in remission - any new OTC helps out there? First, so sorry to hear you've had a recurrence of IC symptoms after all this time. That really stinks! As for you taking Naprosyn...this is an NSAID. I was under the impression that this type of medication is not tolerated well by ICers which may be why your bladder didn't like it years ago when you were having symptoms on a regular basis. Perhaps your bladder just couldn't take it any longer. I have pretty bad arthritis and often wish I could take an anti-inflammatory pain medication...but I can't. It would trading one pain for another. Of course whether the Naprosyn is the culprit is hard to say for sure. I don't know how you've been eating over the past five years. If you reverted back to your pre-IC eating habits...that could eventually catch up with you as well. For me, the usual medications for burning never worked. I tended to use ice packs for burning and hot compresses for bladder pain. Basically...whatever worked. It may be that you just have to ride this out and with the adherence to the IC diet and maybe cutting out the Naprosyn, that may be enough to get you back on the right track. I hope someone here can offer you some useful information. I sure hope you're feeling better soon!
signature: ************************************************************** Experienced first symptoms in 2007 Diagnosed with IC 6/13 Diagnosed with PFD (Pelvic Floor Dysfunction) 8/13 Medications: .5 mg. Ativan, occasionally for spasms Supplements: DH Aloe Vera capsules, 3 per day for maintenance and 6-12 per day during flares Following Strict IC Diet

contents: Re: Ugh, flaring after years in remission - any new OTC helps out there? Lots of new things to help pain... particularly Pro Sirona, an essential oil from the Marigold plant that helps to reduce pelvic for endometriosis. Some IC'ers have found it very helpful! Also, though these don't fight pain but they may help reduce bladder inflammation over the long term thus reducing pain, etc... have you considered Cysto Renew or CystoProtek for their bladder coating effects? Also... some patients reported that CystoProtek has helped improve joint discomfort because of the glucosamine & chondroitin. You can find all of those in our shop at: http://www.icnsales.com Jill

contents: Re: Ugh, flaring after years in remission - any new OTC helps out there? Unfortunately, anti-inflammatory medications can be a problem for an IC bladder --- so it's possible that's what has brought you back to having symptoms again. Cystoprotek is a new over the counter remedy that I'm seeing posts that it's helping. You will find it in the ICN Shop. Sticking with the diet and drinking enough are other things that could help. If you remember what you did to bring yourself into remission, you could try that again. That's one reason it can help to keep a record of treatments, etc., --- even if it's just a note on a calendar, such as "started (name of med)" on the specific date --- and hopefully "feeling better" on another date. Sending well wishes, Donna

contents: Re: Ugh, flaring after years in remission - any new OTC helps out there? I have 2 thoughts: I am also menopausal and had frequency and burning. I went to a gynecologist who specializes in menopause, she tested my blood and found my estrogen was way too low. I am now on vaginal estrogen creme and am feeling much better, no more burning or frequency. I could not take commercial estrogen because of the chemicals in them so I go to a compounding pharmacy and get my estrogen compounded in coconut oil. Are you taking any hormone therapy? You may want to consider it, it really helped me. Second, my husband has psoriatic arthritis and had gotten to the point he could hardly walk. Now he is on Enbrel and it has been a miracle drug for him. I know people with rheumatoid arthritis who has had good results with Humira and Enbrel. I also remember someone posting on this forum that one of these drugs help their IC also. I will try to find that post and see which drug they took. Jo Ann

Re: Ugh, flaring after years in remission - any new OTC helps out there? There is an earlier thread about Humira causing IC to go into remission and there was a trial study on this. You can find the thread by doing a search for Humira. Jo Ann

Re: Ugh, flaring after years in remission - any new OTC helps out there? It is not surprising that biologic drugs like Humira and Remicade would work for IC. They use these medications for treating Crohn's disease which is almost equivalent to IC ( they both cause chronic inflammation and ulcerations and bleeding). I will say that I highly doubt any doctor would prescribe them for IC though. There are very high stakes when taking biologic meds. They can cause an array of serious side effects ( even cancer). I however in my opinion believe that they should look into it because if it could help some people who are not getting any results with their current IC treatments then it would at least be an option for them.

Re: Ugh, flaring after years in remission - any new OTC helps out there? I was discussing Humira for the original poster because she has rheumatoid arthritis and could not take the drug she had been prescribed for RA because it made her IC flare. I guess I was not clear, but I wonder if she took Humira for her RA would that also help her IC. I know Enbrel has been a miracle drug for my husband who has Psoriatic Arthritis, but he (fortunately) does not have IC. I know that the biologics have side effects (my husband has not really had any side effects yet, he has been on Enbrel 4 years) . If these drugs could help people with IC for whom nothing else helps, it seems to me a good trade off. It would be better than living a life of pain.

Re: Ugh, flaring after years in remission - any new OTC helps out there? I agree. Re: Ugh, flaring after years in remission - any new OTC helps out there? Thanks so much everyone! I'm so happy to know that there are things I can try. I love this resource, and so appreciate the information and the compassion and understanding.
Re: Ugh, flaring after years in remission - any new OTC helps out there? Let us know what you try and how it works out, it may help someone in the future. Jo Ann

Re: Ugh, flaring after years in remission - any new OTC helps out there? Yes, please let us know what helped. I am flaring after a 2 year remission. I suspect my diet was most likely the culprit because I was eating.....like a normal person! I am on Elmiron, which helped me go into remission but I had lowered down to 200mg these last couple of years. I just titrated back up to 400mg to see if it will help me with the flare. I had been taking Urelle, but my dr. Just gave me some samples of Uribel to try. Those are my life savers! I hope you come out of the flare soon!

Re: Ugh, flaring after years in remission - any new OTC helps out there? I've read there is a new anti inflammatory in the works that may be ok to take specifically for IC. Unfortunately it may not be available until 2016.

Re: Ugh, flaring after years in remission - any new OTC helps out there? Hi Earthlady, Is this the AQX-1125 in trials? Have you heard anyone talk about trying it? I can't seem to find any info... Thanks, Jill
signature: "Where there is hope there can be faith. Where there is faith, miracles can occur."

contents: Re: Ugh, flaring after years in remission - any new OTC helps out there? Yes I think that is the one. I haven't heard of anyone here though mentioning being able to apply for any of the trials. I found it on another bladder health website but don't remember which one.

Does the severity of ic flares get a little better after the first year? Not because of pain meds though, just on its own. I thought I had read this somewhere here a while ago but I could be mistaken. Would love to hear from you.
signature: First diagnosed June, 2014 by MD Confirmed diagnosis July, 2014 by specialist Uro. Current medications: Amitriptyline 150mg Omeprazole 40mg Hydrocodone 10/325 as needed (4x day) Pyridium 200mg as needed (4x day) Armour Thyroid 30mg Diagnosed with IC, Acid reflux and Hypothyroidism. I have mainly urethral burning and pain as well as vaginal pain. No history of bladder infections and did not experience any symptoms of IC until June, 2014 when I was diagnosed. On an elimination diet, found that I do not tolerate preservatives well. I do not eat any prepackaged food unless it is organic and made w/o preservatives. God Bless, Julie

contents: Re: severity of flares over time? Hi Julie, I have never heard that before, but I think a lot of ICers get better control of their flares over time. For example, I act immediately when I feel a flare coming on (uribel, klonopin, amitriptyline, strict IC diet), and it gets better much more quickly than when I was first diagnosed, and let things go on for too long.
signature: Jane Diagnosed 1/7/14 via cysto/hydro Current Treatment: 10 mg amitriptyline, DH Aloe Vera Used During Flares: Uribel, extra DH Aloe Vera, Klonopin and Tramadol (as needed) Practicing bladder training and stress control (hot baths, pelvic stretching) Recently tried PT, do not appear to have PFD Things that have not helped in the past: Oxybutynin, Vesicare, Pyridium, Myrbetriq, Vagifem Also have migraines and IBS

contents: Re: severity of flares over time? I think it depends on the person individually. I never had "flares". When my bladder pain started, it was consistent for about 2 years straight. I went on antibiotics and my pain was nearly gone. I had to go off the antibiotics due to side effects and my pain came back. I then am on Neurontin and that has helped but now I am having side effects to that as well. My bladder is not great right now. I am not sure, but I think it depends on what is causing the IC to begin with in terms of whether IC gets better or worse over time.

Re: severity of flares over time? I suspects what happens is that we learn how to recognize a flare when it's first coming on --- so we immediately take action to stop it. My flares these days are not very often, and they don't last long because at the very first twinge, I start treating it. Warm hugs, Donna

contents: Re: severity of flares over time? Mine has got worse during 4 years.

Re: severity of flares over time? Over 2 years my flares have gotten much worse but my good days are much better. There is a much clearer contrast between the 2. When I was 1st diagnosed I felt pretty much the same all the time.
signature: Diagnosed by cystoscopy Dec 2012 after 1st baby Was doing well, had 2nd baby and enjoyed 4 months of remission with breastfeeding, I ate everything, it was great! IC came back in July 2014, have been stable again for 2 years. I am now struggling badly with IBS-D. What seems to help me: Elmiron 3x per day. Cimetidine - no longer take this, it may have contributed to GI problems DH Aloe Cornsilk - Drink this as a tea occasionally Diet and prelief - although i'm not very diet sensitive I still stick to a friendly diet and when I do eat something iffy I take prelief and have very little problems, I rarely flare with food. Amitryptaline Probiotics, I am not taking these right now due to GI issues Lots of water Heat Panadol (I think this is Tylenol in the States) Sleep

contents: Re: severity of flares over time? Some people find their IC can worsen during the first few years when it's still developing, but nearly all will level off after that and most will find improvement once we find what helps and what harms. Donna

contents: I'm on vacation in Italy right now, it's my first day here. I've been looking forward to this trip for so long. But this week has been hell - pain and insane frequency. Is there any way I can keep my head up and enjoy this trip? I don't want to bring my family down. But imagining doing everything while feeling this way is so depressing. I can't imagine having to, like, wait in line for hours at the Vatican while having to pee and being in pain. There is not a single minute where I don't feel this discomfort. It just feels so unfair. I'm on the verge of tears all the time and I don't want to ruin this trip for everyone else. Has anyone else been able to have fun while experiencing symptoms like this? I'm been taking tons of pyridium, bladder ease, cystoprotek, baking soda in water and nothing's helping. I even took a course of Cipro because urgent care thought I had a uti but then I saw a urologist who said I didn't have a uti. I've never been super officially diagnosed as ic but I've been having symptoms like this for a long time, at least since 2011. Is there any way to work through this? Anyone have any luck just...trying to forget about these symptoms and trying to have a good time?

Re: On vacation Are you following the IC diet at all? Do you know anything about the water you're drinking, if it has added minerals or what the PH might be? I imagine it must be difficult for you being on vacation in a country known for it's excellent food so far away from home, but it's possible that your bladder is sensitive to what you're eating and drinking. Unfortunately, I don't know of anything you can take to immediately decrease frequency. Urgency and frequency is something I struggle with myself, even when I'm not in a flare. I'm sorry you're suffering and hope you get to feeling well enough to enjoy your vacation with your family. Last edited by Bladderella; 10-11-2014, 05:26 AM.
signature: "When you gotta go, you gotta go!"

contents: Re: On vacation I was just going to say the very same thing. Are you following an IC diet? If not, please begin today. You'll find a link to the food list in my signature below. If you don't have access to a printer, please do select some things from the bladder friendly column and stick to those things at least while you're on vacation. You might want to take a day off to just rest and give yourself time to take it easy. You could also try to find a wheelchair so you can sit instead of standing in line --- or a walker with a seat. Warm hugs, Donna

Re: On vacation I have no advice...just wanted to say I'm so sorry your long awaited vacation is starting this way. And I imagine following the IC diet in Italy wouldn't be an easy task. I hope you get through it.
