contents: So.. I am graduating this spring, but going on a travel abroad class for a month right afterward. We're going to Japan- taking a flight, and then lots of train rides. Apparently, there are bathrooms on bullet trains, but maybe not on other trains? I also hear its common to come by bathrooms that don't have toilet paper, so I know I have to keep tissues on hand. Does anyone have any other tips? I am so afraid I'll get a flare from/while riding on a train or something. Our prof said we could be on a regular train for as long as an hour. YIKES. This, on top of being vegan and trying to be sure the meals don't have oranges and such in them... it's going to be tough, for sure.

surviving Japan Hi, I went to Japan and before I went I get all my meds lined up. I took prosed/ds (a combo drug with an anti-spasm component, anti-infective, and mild bladder analgesic, it makes your pee blue, ), 2 or 3 times a day and at night I took valium. I slept OK and avoided flares this way. I even drank green tea and ate green tea ice-cream, but the pills really helped. I also think the fact that there was not lots of fresh fruit around (maybe a few slices in a hotel) kept me from being tempted. We took the bullet train, trains and subways, and I'm pretty sure that the regular trains had bathrooms, at least I could not have lasted if they hadn't. The bathrooms are such a trip there - either they are primative and you have to squat (but to tell you the truth supposedly that is better for you because the pelvic muscles are in a better position to pee) or they are seated style - usually all seated ones have a "bidet" spray and some public ones in fancy areas have buttons with a musical note that makes a flushing sound for privacy! I was worried I wouldn't find enough toilets, but I did, even on a mountain, even though it was primative. One hotel room had a toilet room with a little sink on top using the clean water that was going to flush it for washing your hands, and little slippers only for use in that tiny room. The subways underground are huge with acres and acres of passages filled with stores and restaurants with those plastic foods in the windows. I found bathrooms OK there. Just remember if you go anywhere rustic that you may have to squat or stand to pee and adjust you clothing accordingly, but most hotels and urban areas have the Western style. Just make sure you get medicine that will control your pain, medicine for sleep which is so vital, and emergency antibiotic if you are prone to infection - ask your doc. Plan ahead and you will have a better time. Don't drink a lot of fluid until you know you will be around bathrooms for awhile. Careful on the plane and standing in line for customs, etc. Good luck and let us know how it goes. PV
signature: I got IC in 1970! I was not diagnosed until 1991. I've tried many drugs and therapies but I tend to only resort to drugs when in a flare because when I am not in a flare (from being good on diet), I suffer only from small bladder volume (like about 7 ozs.) and peeing will relieve the discomfort. When I am feeling relatively normal, I say to myself I am glad I am not on a drug. When I am in a flare, I say, why am I not on a drug! I've recently have been trying to solve my connective issue problems in general. I look to diet and herbs mostly unless it gets really bad. I still think there is great hope for each individual finding a path to healing and there are many.

contents: TY so much. I'm so scared but we have mandatory doctor visits before we go, so I will definitely bring some of these things up to my doctor, as I am only taking elavil currently. It helps, but I still get really awful flares if I eat something wrong (tonight: a non-alcoholic pina colata. MISTAKE.) or due to hormones... so... yes. Thanks so much for the tips.

Purpleviolet gave you some great tips. I am originally from Japan. I moved to the US in 2003 to be with my husband. I don't know where you are going to fly from. You probably have to change airplane couple of times to get to Japan. ( domestic fright to international fright) One thing that you really need is, you should probably get a doctor's document that states your condition so if you need to use restrooms in public places, such as airport or in the airplane restroom. That may be very helpful for you. make sure that you get an aisle seat near the restroom especially long fright to Japan so you have an easy access to the restroom. Waiting line in the airplane can be tough. I don't know if you are sensitive about any toilet paper. Usually, toilet papers on the plane are rough. I always have my toilet paper just in case that I really have to go. I go to the bathrooms so frequently,and sometimes I get toilet paper burn from wiping too much. So for me, SCOT tissue or equivalent tissue are NO, NO. also, avoid trigger food in the airplane.Having a flare in the airplane is a nightmare!! So if you know which food is not good for you, please don't eat them. Making sure if you have comfy shoes such as slippers in your carry-on bags to relax. Going to Japan is really tiring thing so make sure that you have comfy shoes and clothes. You will be sitting in the seat for at least 12-14 hours (from east cost to Tokyo). If you need any more information, just email me. Last edited by Sunflower2; 03-27-2010, 03:01 PM. Reason: typo
signature: Aya IC Diagnosed in September, 2005 My conditions are : IC, PFD, Allergies, and Constipation "It's not easy being grateful all the time. But it's when you feel least thankful that you are most in need of what gratitude can give you." — Oprah Winfrey

contents: Thanks a lot, Sunflower! We do have a very long journey ahead- from Upper Peninsula of Michigan to Detroit, Michigan, and then straight to Tokyo, where we take a train to Hikone! Thanks for the tips, that will be helpful!

What about eating there? Purpleviolet and Sunflower, (those names go together nicely!) what did you eat while you were in Japan? The last time I was there was 2005. My mom is Japanese and we were staying with her relatives. At that time I did not have IC. I have been wondering ever since I got IC if I could ever go to Japan again. My main problem is frequency, but unless I'm flaring I can usually hold it for a long time. If I went to Japan now, I'd probably still be okay if staying with family, but I don't know how I'd handle eating out. It's hard enough to do it here. I'm familiar enough with Japanese food but how do you handle restaurant situations, for example?

food I think my pills really controlled my reactions. Before you go is the time to test the medicine you take (and some Japanese food). I did not find the food particularily irritating for some reason. Not a lot of tomato stuff (which bothers some IC'ers). There was fish and fish broth and always rice. For some reason the bottled ice tea was OK from vending machines , but that may not be the case with you. Then just stick with water. Try at home miso soup, etc and see how you react. I'm not sure if food had msg or if that is reactive. If you eat poultry or pork or shrimp, the fried versions (kantasu (sp?)) seemed OK and so was the tempura. Then there was weird fishy stuff I was served on some preplanned meals I had no control over. Hotels you could eat rice and eggs and miso if you can tolerate that. I think you need to know your own triggers and if you are scared just stick to the plainest food you can find. Street vendors always sell vanilla soft ice cream, too. PV

contents: Purpleviolet, thanks for the food tips. I do okay with soy-seasoned stuff, but I am cautious and when I have them now, I don't do them again until like a week later. I am thinking that if I ever go to Japan again, I might get some kind of card or something printed up in Japanese, saying I have a medical condition, etc., like our IC restroom access cards. Treehugger, enjoy your trip to Japan. I pray that your IC behaves during your travels -- Japan is a fun and fascinating place that begs to be explored and enjoyed.

I don't know how IC is called in Japan, but I am sure there is a name for it. If you are thinking about bring your medical document to Japan, I can probably translate it for you so the people in Japan will know your condition. Let me know if you need anything else. Aya
signature: Aya IC Diagnosed in September, 2005 My conditions are : IC, PFD, Allergies, and Constipation "It's not easy being grateful all the time. But it's when you feel least thankful that you are most in need of what gratitude can give you." — Oprah Winfrey

contents: Arigato Sunflower, that is very kind of you. About a year ago I happened to see that the ICA website had links to information written in Japanese. I will print that out one of these days; I just haven't done so yet because I don't have a printer, but my boyfriend can do it from his workplace. At one point I actually made my mother sit in front of my computer and read it because I was frustrated trying to explain IC to her. (She speaks English but is not so good with understanding medical talk.) I hope when Treehugger returns from Japan she will post here about how she managed during her trip.

Hi guys, sorry, I suck at this forum thing. We're leaving tomorrow :O I've purchased one of the IC-friendly cushions from the store, and I got a messenger bag so I can take it around when we ride in trains/buses/taxis. My Dr. gave me some pyridium to try before the trip, but it didn't seem to do much for me so I'm not bringing it- just bringing my elavil. This trip will be doubly interesting because not only do I have IC, but I'm vegan ;P so I am also bringing prelief. I'm going to look for those ICA pages in Japanese.. that might be helpful. I bought some of the cards that say I have a medical condition, but of course they are only in English. :P Anywho, we (my gf and I) have already started blogging the trip and we hope to continue doing so. I did decide to mention IC in the posts... I think it's important to spread awareness. It's such an awkward thing to explain to people, but it's got such a big impact on our lives. People should know. Here's the blog: http://spiritedawayjapan.blogspot.com/ I'll post when I come back too! Thanks for all the info, guys

Oh, here's a page I found- it's an all-Japanese site about IC. I printed out several pages of it. You can copy and paste the text into yahoo Babelfish to translate it (vaguely, anyway) and see if it says what you need it to say. http://www.tomonoki.org/

Hi again, everyone! I'm back from Japan. I had a pretty easy time, IC-wise. I had a bit of trouble right away when we were traveling TO the country because I had never flown before and was already nervous, then we took a PROP PLANE D: it was terrifying, so I had the nerves, so I had a minor flare. Other than that, my first two weeks were pretty much fine because I was careful with what I ate. There were bathrooms pretty much EVERYWHERE! In the trains, at the shrines, downtown, etc. There was never a time when I couldn't find a bathroom using the phrase "otoire wa doko des ka?" The third week, we did homestays. I think my hormones kicked in and caused me to have a miserable first few days. I also had less control over my diet so I wasn't always exactly sure what I was eating (other than it being mostly vegan). Again, always found bathrooms when we were out, I was just peeing a lot at that time. The second half of the week was fine, and the flight home was no problem. I bought the expensive cushion from the shop and it was useless to me. I am so sorry I bought it and brought it with me- not only did it do nothing, but it took up a ton of space in my carry-on. The biggest worry while we were there was when we went to our host family's grandparents house. Our "grandmother" didn't know what to feed us since my gf and I are vegan, so she plopped down a ton of fruit in front of us. She also made onigiri (rice balls) for us, which had pickled plum inside. I found out during the trip that pickled plum (umeboshi) is hated by my bladder. So I was eating grapes, umeboshi, banana, and.. she put a huge orange in front of both of us. Fortunately, my host mother explained that I couldnt have fruit juice (though I guess grandma-san didn't make the connection about the orange being.. fruit.. also..).. but we were full before we got to the orange anyway. ;D AND I had prelief.. I took 3 tablets out of sheer panic, because we had driven out of town and were walking to a shrine ROFL it could have been a disaster.. but I was fine. Anywho, it wasn't a huge problem thankfully. Thought I'd report back.

Thanks for the update! Treehugger, I'm glad your IC wasn't too bad during your trip. Weird that you had to board a prop plane... were you traveling to somewhere remote? I'm assuming that since you found an abundance of bathrooms, that you must have encountered some of the Japanese toilets with all the bells and whistles. While I was there, at my aunt's house, I decided to randomly start pressing buttons while on the toilet... big mistake! I got quite a surprise from one of them... and then there are the old-school toilets that are built into the floor... but I digress. Welcome home!

Pucca- I actually live in Michigan's Upper Peninsula, which is pretty remote! The closest airport is half an hour's drive away, and I'm fairly certain it's one of the only airports in the U.P.. So we had to take that to Detroit, then, because our stupid prop plane was 3 hours LATE, we missed out flight to Narita, so our class was all split up. My girlfriend and I were flown to San Fran on a slightly larger plane, then we spent the night, then took an even larger plane out the next day to Narita. Looooong travel.. for sure. I was feeling awful for the first week due to vertigo and other maladies related to the tough travel. The flight home was 100% better. I felt the pit toilets were actually pretty easy to use! I avoided all the fancy buttons on the western-style toilets at all costs My host family had a brand new toilet that raises it's own lid when you walk into the toilet room, and sure enough it had a ton of buttons. I only employed the flush button, though. No bidet or butt shower for me ROFL

Wow, sounds like the toilets have progressed even more since 2005! I can't imagine a toilet lifting its own lid -- kinda like it's waving at you! I remember that some actually played music... that really cracked me up. Thanks for making me chuckle!

I have been in pain for nearly 5 week since my hydro distention. I got a urinary infection from the surgery so I finally eradicated that with Cipro (Keflex and Augmentin did not help). I was OK for a week and then I began flaring like crazy again (this last week has been a nightmare). I thought maybe it was another UTI but my urine is clear of infection. I finally got copies of my paperwork from the day of my surgery (hydro) and was horrified to see that they stretched my urethra when I told them not to. I believe that is what is causing my spasms right now and they are out of control. That and my bowels. I can feel them both spasming and that's when I flare the most. I have thrown everything at this one and nothing helps. Elavil (upped my dosage), Hydroxyzine, Percocet, Pyridium, Cystoprotek, Bladder Ease, Aloe Vera capsules and even Nuerontin (and trust me there are more). I was starting to panic today thinking I am back to square one after this last hydro and that it hurt me instead of helping me. I kept thinking that nothing would get me out of pain and then I remembered what my first urologist swore by who originally diagnosed me back in California. I called my GP and they called in some Librax for me and I could not believe it. After taking just one tonight, it took the spams down several notches. Now nothing and I do mean nothing has been able to do that for days. I took another one and it almost feels like I am normal again (almost but not all the way there). Now that is a miracle for me. Nothing else has been able to even put a dent in this flare. With all the newer drugs and stuff I had forgotten about Librax totally. My doctor even admitted that it is the best stuff out there because it halts all spasms completely. It stops both your colon and your bladder from spasming. No wonder I feel better. Why did I allow myself to forget this drug! I have always thought that my flares were caused by spasms in my colon and my bladder because even a pinch of gas can make my bladder flare. This calms the storm on both ends at once and leaves your nerves calmed down by the Librium part of the drug. I feel like an idiot because it was the only thing that helped me back in the eighties and I literally forgot about it with all the new stuff they throw at us. I just wanted to share this because its definitely something you guys might want to try if nothing else is helping you. My flares are literally spasm related. Its like everything is just buzzing and I am practically clenching my teeth. Librax is like a miracle! I am taking (1) 2.5 Librax three times a day and I have to say it knocks Elavil out of the water. Thank God...some relief finally!! I can't believe what a ding a ling I have been in forgetting about that drug! It works better than Percocet! I just put in a request with my old urologist (I will not be returning to the new one ever again) for Librax on a daily basis like I have been doing with the Elavil. I pray he says yes because this is a Godsend here. Just thought I would share for whatever its worth. Stops the painful spasms right in their tracks (I think that many of us are actually spasming from our colons too and just don't realize that we have two things going at once so this could be a godsend for you too). All I know is that I am grateful that something is working. I was beginning to panic after my 4th sitz bath today and all these drugs. God I hate this disease. It really is a very cruel one.

contents: Yes it is a cruel disease for sure! I am so pleased you found some relief! Thank you for passing this information along to us. I'm going to do a search on Librax. I have not heard of it before. That is not unusal for me. I learn something every day from nice people like you that take the bother to share. I hope you continue to feel better each day. Hugs, Ziggy
I'm glad you found something that helped you. Take Care

contents: In the summer I had a UTI after a hydro/ cysto and had to take 2 course of Ab's for it, it then moved to my kidneys, and of course you know what this was doing to my IC which was really well uncontrol, after this I was bit by a dog and had to take Ab's agin right away, by this time my bowels were in a total upheaval I thought from all the Ab's but my Dr. thought I had picked up a bowel infection too, he had given my all the anti spasam med but they cause me retention which I could not handle on top of everything else. After a week of a different kind of Ab I was feeling a little bit better and so my Dr. gave me another week of AB and librium for the spasams and yes it did work well for me, but my whole system was so messed up I didn't realize it helpped the bladder too, I will remeber this. It still took a couple more weeks after this for me to start to feel any where near normal again. It was a h**ish summer for my health. I will keep this med in mind if I need that kind of power again. I also agree that bowels can cause flares, if my bowles get upset I flare too. I am told they are all the same kind of muscles so when one spasams often both will. MG
signature: My are with you all. May you all find a way to peace and joy in your lives.

contents: I'm honestly and sincerely blown away by the way that Librax has taken me completely out of pain. I don't know whether to laugh cry because all this time this drug was there. It smooths out the muscles and stops contractions in the bowel and bladder so everything is at peace for once. I haven't had this kind of pain relief for a very long time. Librax works better than Percocet or anything I have ever tried. This is unreal for me. I am not having trouble with retention and actually had some loose stools (sorry to be a bit graphic) which surprised me because I think this drug can contribute to constipation. For me that's a small thing to deal with next to the pain I have been going through. Haven't heard back yet from my Urologist in Maryland, (not going back to that idiot in PA ever again) but praying he says OK because I swear this is the best drug I have ever used. It stops all movement dead in its tracks. I'm just excited to have something...anything that can even remotely get me out of this pain. Its actually exhausting to be in this all the time. I can never sit for long. I have to lay on my side in bed watching TV because I can't have any pressure on my bladder or that area. It gets old and frustrating. I miss having sex with my husband but Its just so painful. Librax does make me feel like I can almost pretend I am normal for a while and that's the first pain med I have had that reaction from in a while. It takes those painful spasms away from both sides. Crossing my fingers that my doctors OKs it. Found this very interesting. I am one of those that has both IBS and IC so no wonder! WEBSITE.WS - Your Internet Address For Life™ http://www.irritable-bowel-syndrome.ws/librax.htm

contents: Aww thank you Donna. I feel the same way. Why I didn't think about Librax sooner I'll never know. I had a major revelation today with my husband over breakfast because it never dawned on me before. Years I ago I had a hydro that I thought was the reason I was in remission for so many years. In fact I thought my IC was gone and would never come back (in those days we didn't know much about IC). Everytime I would bring that up to a doctor, they would always tell me that they had never heard of that before. That if a Hydro works at all, it will only put people in remission for short periods of time (not years). What I had totally forgotten about is that at the same time I had that distention, my doctor stopped the opiates I was on because they were not working and gave me Librax. I never made the connection until now (I was on it for a while). When I read that page that I linked to above in my other post where people said that Librax put them in remission for their IBS and stuff like that, a major lightbulb went off. It was Librax all along that did that for me. I feel it in my gut and can't believe how I overlooked it. I have a feeling this colonoscopy next week is going to reveal some problems and thats why the Librax helped me so much before and is making most of the pain go away now. The bowel and bladder are so close together. This could be the miracle I've been praying for. All I know is that this drug seems to be taking out nearly all the pain and suffering for me. The only problem is that it causes constipation (noticing that now today and of course I will have to stop using this a day or two before my colonoscopy). I ordered a bunch of stuff from the ICN store here (including Heather's Acacia gum). Hopefully that will help to counter balance things a bit. If I have to, I can drink a dose of Phillips MOM evey other day or so because that works like a charm for me. Just so happy to finally have a drug that truly knock this pain out!

contents: Hi I just wanted to say I have found that Miralax works wonders for my and our pain seems to be simular. It is an OTC med but I did dicuss taking this full time with my Dr. he said it was fine, the bottle I think says for 2 weeks, so you may want to dicuss this with your Dr. too and give it a try. It has helpped the pain and cramping of IBS so much for me, and therefore also helpped the IC spsasams too. Just an other option to consider. Hope your colonoscopy goes well for you the prep is the worst part of it. MG
signature: My are with you all. May you all find a way to peace and joy in your lives.

contents: ICP, I find that quite interesting (Librax) I'm going to reseach it and see if I maybe can take it. I also have horrific colon spasms. I don't have to tell you how much they hurt. I would rather have a baby. And then everything down there starts to hurt and spasm. Have you ever been told why? My pt thinks it was the interstim I had at one time that had a big kick start of them. I use to have them every time I had a BM when I had the stim. They are just plain horrible!!!! Just wanted to add, I saw that one of the side affects is difficulty urinating. Has this happened to you? Hugs and blessings Last edited by maryla; 10-21-2009, 05:45 AM.
signature: MARY Serenity isn't freedom from the storm.....it's peace within the storm.....I had my sick bladder removed Jan 7th 2010.....Even though I had many complications.......I would do it again in a moment.....I have no regrets......Sunflowers in memory of my sister who passed March 14th 2010......they were her favorite....

contents: Mary, its been an amazing drug for me. Its actually prescribed for severe colon spasms which is why my urologist told me he would have never thought about that drug (its usually prescribed by a Gastroenterologists). In fact he said I gave him something to think about with some of his tougher cases where nothing gives any relief. He told me that he may try Librax with a few of his other patients. My first urologist many years ago swore by it. We kept trying to attack the pain with several opiates (none worked). We tried stuff for bladder burning, but again nothing worked. The drugs out there that help with bladder spasms only make it worse because you can't pee and they are not that effective anyway for pain (by the way Librax did not cause any notable retention in me). He believed that the bladder and colon spasm at the same time in women with IC, which is why he gave it to me. It seems to take the burn away and everything. Its one of those things where you scratch me there, but I am really itching here. In other words it may feel like just a bad burning sensation when really it could be all coming from non stop spasming as well. You attack the pain from a different angle. Instead of just trying to take down the burn, you stop the endless spasming that is caused by both the bladder and the bowel. I have always known that most of my pain comes from spasms. None of the so called urological spasmodics have ever done a thing for me. They just cause horrible retention and never seemed to work (and that's probably because my colon was still spasming and so I felt little relief to have my bladder calm down with that still going). This drug halts spasms of both the colon and the bladder also giving you a little Librium in there to help calm the nerves and the bladder. This drug is also a muscle relaxer so it smooths contractions. Its a real wonder pill as far as I'm concerned. I just feel so stupid for forgetting all about it as I was on it for a few years back in the eighties and it helped me tremendously. The bladder and bowel are very close together. Its also given me time to reflect because I had my first hydro at the same time he started me on Librax and after a while I went into a remission for many years (which I hear is unheard of). The hydros that followed did nothing for me whatsoever. I kept thinking why did that one put me in remission for so many years and the others do not even give me a month. I am beginning to believe it may have been the Librax because in retrospect that makes a lot more sense now when I think of the timing and how the other hydro's did nothing. Every urologist I have seen has said that no hydro puts you in remission for years and years. When I look back, I was on Librax for a few years straight and that may have indeed been the thing that put me in remission. When I read all those posts at that link from people with severe IBS who said that Librax actually put them in remission and gave them back their lives, it clicked. I was on two librax four times a day for quite some time so I broke the cycle of pain long enough for things to calm down. I notice that at times I almost feel normal with Librax so that's a sign of hope for me. I hope it can help you. I know its helped me tremendously. P.S. Its very common to have IBS and IC at the same time. IBS will make your colon spasm like crazy (and IC makes your bladder spasm like crazy). This kills them both at once. I usually feel major relief after my second dose each day. Its an anticholergenic so it can dry you out a bit (makes my nose a little dry, but I just use saline spray and stuff like that). You may get constipation, but there are many things to help deal with that and so far it has not affected me badly either way. Last edited by ICPrincess; 10-22-2009, 09:29 AM.

So I was going about 4 x's a day. My bladder felt so much better!! If I have even a little gas or I can't go # 2 or feel a little constipated, my bladder automatically acts up. I also feel that my IC is triggered by colon spasms. I have never heard of Librax. Thanks.

contents: Talk about getting chills! I read what you just said and thought it was just so ironic as that's exactly what happens to me to a T! On days when I am easily going number 2, my IC is at its best. I have either no flare or much less of a flare. On days when I am constipated or have any gas at all, those are my worst IC days always. I have actually told all my urologists this. I actually take Phillips Milk OF Magnesia (liquid only as the pills do nothing for me) to break a bad flare because I was constipated and knew it would take my pain down. It always does. It releives all the gas and gets me going within two or three hours with no cramping and soon the flare has died down considerably. Any pressure from the bowel on my bladder causes me enormous pain. It feels like a bad engine in a car or something that is rumbling and making everything else rumble with it (like the bladder is to the bowel). My first urologist always believed that the connection was a strong one and that the bowel and bladder are very closely related in more ways than one. It was the reason I decided to go ahead and have the colonoscopy I just went through last week. They did not find anything serious (only small polyps which were luckily benign as they just called today to let me know). They were Hyperplastic polyps and again very small, but I do have IBS and so do many IC patients. My bowel literally dictates what type of IC day I will have. I am about to try Heather's infamous Acacia Fiber as far as constipation goes and if I have to, I will go to Prune Juice in small amounts even though I know its not particularly IC friendly (or as Mothergoose suggested, maybe do the Miralax on a more regular basis as even in large doses like the one I had to ingest for my colonoscopy prep, I got no cramping or pain or anything). I now use the Phillips as a rescue type thing to get me out of bad flares that I know are totally bowel related. When my bowel spasms, my bladder spasms and I can barely walk. Its a horrible feeling. One gets the other going and unless the cycle is broken, the flare continues, so its a matter of stopping the spasming as opposed to numbing the pain or taking something for the burning sensation, which comes with the spasms. I really believe that's why so many women have really long lasting flares. Opiates can't truly touch spasms (they can't reach deep enough to numb the actual source of the pain) .They can numb your mind a bit so that they are not as intense, but they are still there because the cycle has not been broken. You have to stop the spasms to get things to die down and Librax is a miracle in that respect. Once you have something to knock out both the bowel and the bladder from spasming, everything else works so much better. Interestingly enough my new Gastro said he is not surprised at all. That bladder and bowel spasms usually go hand in hand and yet most Urologists are somehow clueless about this. If you get a urologist who is unwilling to try the Librax (which is really not considered heavy artillery like the opiates and stuff), try a gastro and tell them you have severe IBS that is causing spasms in both your bladder and bowel and want to try Librax as it has helped others you know (that's always a good way to get them a little more open to the idea). We could be twins with yoru description there! Its sad that we know so much more than our own doctors about this disease. In fact I have found that some internists are better at understanding IC than urologists. Often times family doctors will give you prescriptions like that easier than a urologist. Some even seem to know more about the disease than the urologists do, which is very disappointing. Last edited by ICPrincess; 10-22-2009, 02:45 PM.

contents: Originally posted by ICPrincess I'm honestly and sincerely blown away by the way that Librax has taken me completely out of pain. I don't know whether to laugh cry because all this time this drug was there. It smooths out the muscles and stops contractions in the bowel and bladder so everything is at peace for once. I haven't had this kind of pain relief for a very long time. Librax works better than Percocet or anything I have ever tried. This is unreal for me. I am not having trouble with retention and actually had some loose stools (sorry to be a bit graphic) which surprised me because I think this drug can contribute to constipation. For me that's a small thing to deal with next to the pain I have been going through. Haven't heard back yet from my Urologist in Maryland, (not going back to that idiot in PA ever again) but praying he says OK because I swear this is the best drug I have ever used. It stops all movement dead in its tracks. I'm just excited to have something...anything that can even remotely get me out of this pain. Its actually exhausting to be in this all the time. I can never sit for long. I have to lay on my side in bed watching TV because I can't have any pressure on my bladder or that area. It gets old and frustrating. I miss having sex with my husband but Its just so painful. Librax does make me feel like I can almost pretend I am normal for a while and that's the first pain med I have had that reaction from in a while. It takes those painful spasms away from both sides. Crossing my fingers that my doctors OKs it. Found this very interesting. I am one of those that has both IBS and IC so no wonder! http://www.irritable-bowel-syndrome.ws/librax.htm I'm so glad to hear you got relief and I'm going to be checking this Librax out for my 10yr old son and I. He also has stomach issues along with the IC. Thank you for sharing!

I really hope it helps him. In the link I posted above some posts up, people talk about how this drug has given them back their lives. All I know is that it may be the first sign of hope I've had in many years. I feel like a schumck for not thinking about it because they had me on it in the eighies, but I thought it was the hydro that put me in remission for all those years (as I said before, now I am not so sure and think it was the Librax). Especially since none of the hydro's done afterwards did a thing for me. I did the hydro and then was put on Librax shortly afterwards (so basically at the same time) and gave all that credit for so many years to the hydro. Because some of us have exhausted everything we can think of to help our IC, I honestly think it would be wise to approach it from another angle to see if that's not the final part of the equation.....our bowels. Look at healing the bowel and stopping pain THERE with drugs made for bowel spasms and pain and see if that doesn't make a huge difference. If it doesn't nothing lost, nothing gained however if it does, it may replace one or more of your current meds and give you some real pain relief. If this continues I could see myself cutting out two meds right off the top of my head. It won't heal my IC, but it may stop the vicious cycle of spasming which triggers the most IC pain for me. As an aside, I so sorry to hear your child is in so much pain. Its incredibly unfair to have to start off life with this kind of relentless disease. I'm so sorry and pray that he will feel some relief soon.

contents: I am in a flare. I thought some of it might be due to spasm. I put my thumb up in area behind bladder (You know where that is ladies) and pressed toward the rear being careful to not press directly on bladder. I also switched hands to get both sides better but one side was much more sore than the other and on that same side is where most of the bladder pain is. It felt tight and aftwards that spot remained sore, but the tightness and pain in the urethral area has let up a bit. I think I just did a little trigger point release that worked. Perhaps a gentle stretch can help with a flare that is caused by spasm. I got this idea after reading sasi's post under success thread. PV
signature: I got IC in 1970! I was not diagnosed until 1991. I've tried many drugs and therapies but I tend to only resort to drugs when in a flare because when I am not in a flare (from being good on diet), I suffer only from small bladder volume (like about 7 ozs.) and peeing will relieve the discomfort. When I am feeling relatively normal, I say to myself I am glad I am not on a drug. When I am in a flare, I say, why am I not on a drug! I've recently have been trying to solve my connective issue problems in general. I look to diet and herbs mostly unless it gets really bad. I still think there is great hope for each individual finding a path to healing and there are many.

contents: Thanks PV That sounds like a good tip.

I have a UTI and it does feel better after 36 hrs. on antibiotics, but I can't remember if you can have a flare while treating a UTI. I'm still sore and tender. Is it common to hang on to the "flare" feeling even after a UTI gets better? I don't usually get UTI's. Thanks for any responses. Liz

Yes, it can flare you. Either the UTI itself or the antibiotic can do it. I have had freguent UTI's and usually I do ok after the antibiotics kick in after a few days, sometimes a week. However one time I flared for a whole month about 4/5 weeks after I had one. The infection was gone and everything it was just a bad flare. After that I went back to pretty much normal . Just hang in there, you will get better. Just try and pay close attention , and make sure the antibiotic you are using is actually getting rid of the infection or else you may need to switch it, otherwise, it is probably just flaring some because of the infection.
signature: Diagnosed with IC in 9/2005, Currently doing good, taking breaks from Elmiron, reduced amount.

contents: Just wondering if anyone has some great tips for dealing with life! I've had bladder problems for 30 years but it has recently become considerably worse and more painful. While I am going through the ordeal of finding a doctor with a solution, I still have obligations. I am lucky to not have to work and have a supportive husband. But I'm essentially homebound. For instance, how do you all go sit in the dentist chair for 2 hours without getting up 5 times? (or do you get up 5 times?) I take tramadol but it's not doing it for now. Do you take stronger medication just for occasions like this? Is anyone else homebound? Any tips for when you have to get out? Riding in the car is miserable right now. Just want to add that it is comforting to know that you are all here. Thanks Mary

Mary, It is tough living with this disease. My main symptom is pain but, I do get urgency and frequency when my flare is at its worst. I take prescription Pyridium during those times. Have you tried it? It helps my urgency and frequency by numbing the bladder. I usually take it before bed bc that is the only way I can get to sleep.......take care Dana W.
signature: DanaW "Don't quit when the tide is lowest, For it's just about to turn; Don't quit over doubts and questions, For there's something you may learn."

contents: Thank you Dana. Actually I tried a pyridium yesterday but didn't notice much improvement. Also tried urelle, think that may make it worse. After all these years I thought I was the expert on living a life around bathrooms, but this time I'm stuck.

Sometimes for me, heat can calm the "constant-need-to-pee" feeling, lol. Have you tried the Thermacare Heat Patches?? I love those things, especially when I need to be wireless, like for sleep or work or what have you. Works with the plain old badder pain too!!! Hugs, Tracey

contents: There are times I do take more medication if I am having a flare. I was on the road on Sat. and was having a small flare and I did use more of my meds that day. As far as the dentist. I let them know I may have to have a bathroom break and don't hesitate to stop them if I need to go. We can't help it that we have this disease and I won't let anyone make me feel bad because I have to go to the bathroom. Not only that, they become more aware of how much we can suffer with this disease.

contents: One thing that helps with car rides is to put a folded hand towel between my seat belt & my abdomen. It doesn't interfere with the operation of the seat belt & takes the pressure off... With the dentist I warn them I may need to get up to use the restroom. Also be careful to let them know you may not be okay with anesthetics with epinephrine in them. That can cause a nasty flare. There are other things they can use instead, but they won't last as long, so you may require a second shot... Other things, well I do know where all the bathrooms are at the grocery store, pharmacy, etc. You might look into ordering groceries on line, though it may make label reading difficult, which is necessary on the IC diet... I'm hoping you can find a doctor quickly who can help you. I remember well my frustration being so sick & not finding anyone who could fit me into their schedule, with not guarantee they'd know what to do anyhow. I'll be praying you find someone helpful soon!

contents: Good idea, Kadi. I will definitely try a towel with the seatbeat. Beats sitting there holding it away from you all the time. I could draw a map of the restrooms in this area and grade them for cleanliness and accessibility. It's surprising the number of places you need to go without public restrooms.

I've also had diagnosed IC for thirty years. When I go to the dentist, I still go to the bathroom when I need to and they do understand. And I have said I will make my fortune writing a restroom directory for the State of Oregon --- complete with pictures since I've visited most of them. Another hint for riding in the car --- an IC pillow (available in the ICN Shop) --- and I use a spring clothespin on my seat belt. What you do is pull the seat belt out so it's very loose, then clip the clothespin where the seat belt comes out of the car. If you do have an accident, the clothespin will come off and the seat belt will work just fine. Donna

contents: crying for help to relieve bladder pain. hi, everyone I found out that I have ic 5-6 years ago and it's been a real struggle for me.knowing what to eat because knowing matter what I eat hurts my bladder it didn't matter what it is and still does like eating pears,fuji apples,drinking water.If you have any suggestions let me know please,I'm still learning about this ic stuff and like I said it's been a real struggle for me.thank you. Ellen

Hi, I am 23 years old and have had diagnosed IC for about 2 years. In April, my family, boyfriend and I will be traveling to Thailand for one month. We will be doing quite a bit of domestic traveling - relocating to a new place every week or so. I have been to Thailand 4 times before, but that was before I had IC... I am really excited about the trip, but also feeling quite nervous. I was wondering if anyone had any good IC travel tips? I am especially anxious about domestic traveling, finding IC friendly foods... Thanks!

Well you are talking big travel here. I would use as much bottle spring water as you can. Watch your foods. I imagine that part of the world could use a lot of MSG and soy sauce in food prep. Both are no- no's for me. The plain rice over there would be ok for me. I use stick on heat pads when I am traveling to help with flares. I don't know what the bathroom facilities are like when you are doing your domestic travel but you may want to stock up on some Travel Johns from the ICN shop so you make sure you always have a "bathroom" with you. Make sure to have plenty of your medication with you in the original bottles and maybe talk to your Dr. about "back-up" prescriptions just in case. Most of all, have a great time!

contents: I finally convinced my uro to prescribe an OAB med for me. I had a post-op followup appt today and I told him I needed an something to curtail my frequency or might lose my job. I asked about Detrol and Ditropan and he suggested Ditropan. I pratically had to beg for it but I got the Rx. I sure hope it works. My quality of life will improve greatly if I can stop having to pee every 30-45 minutes thoughout the workday. I can deal with pain somewhat better most days but the constant going to the bathroom makes it very difficult to do my job. Thanks for reading my post.

contents: Ditropan really helps me -- I would be miserable without it. I've been taking 5 mg twice a day for about 4 years. If it helps you, great.

I'm crossing my fingers that it helps you! I go just as frequently as you and it is a real inconvenience on life. Question for anyone out there. I've tried ditropan and oxytrol. contents: Musiclover, I would ask for another one if I were you. I tried Detrol and Vesicare and neither helped me at all, but Ditropan helps me quite a bit. I don't know what the scientific differences are. But there definitely are differences.
signature: ~ Stacey

contents: Ditropan and Detrol LA did nothing for me. In two weeks time Enablex completely blocked me up with retention. Vesicare helped the most but not with frequency....just less pressure feeling. I tried as many as I could but my problem was IC not OAB, I just didn't know it at the time.

Progress Report The Ditropan XL is curbing my frequency ever so slightly. The last 24 hours I am going about once every hour during waking hours as opposed to 2 and 3 times per hour. The real improvment has came in the overnight hours. Last night I only got up once about 3:30 am.:woohoo: It was soooooo nice to get a good nights sleep for a change. Of course I'm not sure how much of this improvement is due to the fact that my waning uti and my recovery from Hunner's surgery. The burning has decreased quite a bit from what it was last weekend. I still had some seriouis burning the first couple trips to bathroom this morning but the last time I went ( about 20 minutes ago) My urethra and bladder almost felt normal. While I know the Ditropan XL want cure all of my symptoms, I feel that things are looking up for me.

contents: Hi Teresa thanks for your expression of happiness for me. I certainly feel very blessed and grateful for how I am feeling. You know I should post my remission story, and my progress of how I became where I am now. It was a journey of self discovery and patience and keeping self confident through the process knowing I would heal with time. Here is a bit of a rundown....I am on NO medication for IC. When first diagnosed almost two years ago (it will be two years this june 2010) I was provided pain killers and elmirion from my doctor. I refused to take them...I wanted more answers. I had never heard of IC and I felt I wanted a better answer, not just a prescription to meds. I was also provided a referral to a urologist (A highly respected one)....I saw him too, and he provided the advice of a bladder distention. He was so kind and compassionate. But I still felt these were not my answers that I wanted. The more I read on bladder distention and their reliability, and how they were truly only helping those 50 percent of the time with their symptoms, and causing flares in some making symptoms worse....I opted to NOT do this either. In the end I made the decision to go to a naturopath. I took her advice and her healing ritual of UNDA drops and they worked for me. THEY stopped my constant peeing of every 10 minutes, and eased up on my bladder pain and pressure. I felt I was on the road to healing. I then started acupuncture, and physio therapy with a trained person in pelvic floor dysfunction dealing with IC patients. She used a new technology on me with infa-red lazer treatment and his was in my opionion what truly brought me to the next level of healing. This machine is normally used on patients with injuries to shoulders or back dealing with inflammation...etc...she used it on my bladder area and vaginal area. IT helped tremendously. As far as meds go, I TOOK none. I did take natural supplements for about 8 months. I took omega 3's, magnesium, glucosimine, aloe vera juice, (a few others I am not remember right now....but will get the names if you are interested)....RIGHT NOW I take only my omega's magnesium, and calcium, and a good probiotic. I am pain free, and it feels amazing, wonderful, makes me want to do cartwheels! It was a long journey, because I really had not support of my doctors at the beginning. They were a bit impatient with me because I did nto want to take the prescription....but they are amazed at pleased now to see where I have progressed on my own. I EAT the IC diet, and I do not take any foods that are irritating. I eat very whole foods, and a lot of raw food diet items. I do not eat anything processed (I never did anyway)...MY DIET is a huge part of my healing. Eating foods very rich in vitamens and nutrients. I do A lot of YOGA, this is a HUGE part of my healing. Being a yoga instructor it was very easy to incorporate this into my lifestyle in order to help ease my pain and heal me to where I am today. This did not happen over night...it took me a little over a year to get where I am today. I hope this helps, and if you have any questions, please feel free to email.
